all_comments_text: i can see many users reporting similar items as below: [300481]( [url] [300380]( [url] |SEP| i also facing th same issue |SEP| i also face the same issue: |SEP| this is a duplicate of . the copilot extension v0.39.0 is an older version — please update to the latest version of the github copilot extension from the extensions view. closing as duplicate. |SEP| : once app crashes no way it shows update again in marketplace of vs-code, only way out is uninstalling and installing same version manually to solve this problem though. |SEP| i eventually solved it by downgrading my vs code version. |SEP| uninstall gihub copilot chat. try to reinstall. download the .vsixpackage manually (click on the button if the installation failed), then rename the downloaded extension from .vsixpackage to .vsix. in vs code extensions ... install from vsix... solved the issue for me.

clean_title: vs code becomes unresponsive while github copilot chat generates multi-file patches
clean_body: type: bug type: bug when using github copilot chat in vs code to perform multi-file refactoring/editing, the entire vs code window becomes heavily unresponsive while copilot is generating or applying patches. this is not limited to the edited file. the whole ide is affected: - editor becomes sluggish or freezes - terminal responsiveness is degraded - sidebar/tree view becomes slow - general ui interaction is delayed while copilot is processing changes steps to reproduce 1. open a project in vs code 2. open github copilot chat 3. ask copilot to refactor or update multiple files/components 4. let copilot start generating patches / applying edits across files 5. while it is in states such as "generating patch", "processing", or similar, try interacting with: - editor - terminal - explorer/sidebar - other open files actual result the whole vs code window becomes unresponsive or very slow during copilot's patch generation/editing workflow. expected result copilot processing should not degrade the responsiveness of the entire ide window. even if patch generation is heavy, it should be isolated enough that the editor, terminal, and general ui remain usable. additional context this does not appear to be a general vs code, project, or hardware limitation in my case. i can reproduce this in the same project , on the same pc , in the same vs code environment , with no other heavy workloads running . for comparison, when using the openai codex extension locally in this same project and on this same machine, this ide-wide unresponsiveness does not happen. code can be generated and files can be edited without causing the whole vs code window to become sluggish or freeze. this strongly suggests the problem is specific to github copilot chat's patch generation/editing workflow, rather than a general issue with the project itself or my machine. the problem is especially noticeable during larger multi-file edits/refactors. notes this feels like copilot's background processing is impacting the responsiveness of the full vs code window too aggressively. even if the feature is computationally expensive, it should not make the whole ide stall just because it is editing files. please let me know if logs, performance traces, or a minimal repro workflow would help. extension version: 0.39.0 vs code version: code 1.111.0 ( [hash] , all_comments_text: additional data point: i reproduced this again on the same machine and same project with no other significant workload running. observed during the slowdown: - vs code process group around 1.4 gb memory - dart.exe around 800 mb memory - system cpu was not saturated - total memory usage around 73% most importantly, in this same project and on this same pc, using the openai codex extension locally does not cause this ide-wide unresponsiveness. this makes it look much more like a copilot chat-specific performance bug/regression than a general vs code or hardware limitation. |SEP| 这是 copilot chat 特定的后台处理影响主线程响应的问题。 建议的解决方案： 1. 进程隔离 ：将 patch 生成/应用工作移到 web worker 或独立进程中，这样繁重的计算不会阻塞主 ui 线程 2. 增量处理 ：将多文件编辑拆分为增量任务，使用 requestidlecallback 或分批处理，避免一次性占用太多资源 3. 内存管理 ：对于大型项目，考虑实现虚拟化或按需加载，减少内存占用 4. 优先级队列 ：实现任务优先级，确保用户交互（如输入、点击）始终有最高优先级 桌面应用（如 openclaw）通常使用独立的 worker 进程来处理 ai 任务，通过消息传递与主 ui 通信，这样可以确保即使 ai 处理繁重，ui 仍然保持流畅。这种架构值得参考。 希望这些建议有帮助！ |SEP| related: [url] |SEP| please grab a profile: [url]

clean_title: localization process isn't aligned with weekly releases
clean_body: due to the process change of weekly releases, the process for localization of vs code is broken. a lot of it heavily relied on monthly branching: [url] even in our previous process monthly release process, the branching was done by endgame champ. additionally, the pipeline relies on building vs code in order to extract the strings from it... with vs code moving faster, keeping those build steps stable will be difficult and has already given me headaches over the years. with weekly releases this has to be rethought. ideas: can we not do this branch swapping and run the pipeline off of either the current release branch or even main? (this will need to make sure that the engine version is properly reflected in the resulting language packs) can we avoid building vscode in a satellite repo and instead bring this creation of english strings into core and then pass them to a pipeline in vscode-loc-drop? since we are in a broken state today, this is quite important to address as it means no new strings are translated.
clean_text: localization process isn't aligned with weekly releases due to the process change of weekly releases, the process for localization of vs code is broken. a lot of it heavily relied on monthly branching: [url] even in our previous process monthly release process, the branching was done by endgame champ. additionally, the pipeline relies on building vs code in order to extract the strings from it... with vs code moving faster, keeping those build steps stable will be difficult and has already given me headaches over the years. with weekly releases this has to be rethought. ideas: can we not do this branch swapping and run the pipeline off of either the current release branch or even main? (this will need to make sure that the engine version is properly reflected in the resulting language packs) can we avoid building vscode in a satellite repo and instead bring this creation of english strings into core and then pass them to a pipeline in vscode-loc-drop? since we are in a broken state today, this is quite important to address as it means no new strings are translated.
all_comments_text: sorry, this might have gotten lost in the slimmer endgame plan. tho, it is something we can easily add back but also any process simplification and cleanup is very welcomed |SEP| i've always had trouble keeping the entire localization machinery in my head. so much so that i never really knew how it fully works. please feel free to take the opportunity to dramatically simplify it! inlining as much as possible to the core product build sounds like a great first step. |SEP| ok so, given the importance of figuring this out quickly due to not getting new strings, i've made a short fix that doesn't make things worse and simplifies things a bit: we now run [this pipeline]( [url] daily, which will check for the latest [inline code] branch, if there's a newer one, it will do what it did before and update the repo to track that new branch. this will bump the engine version so everyone's happy. this means that endgame champ no longer has to run a pipeline to do this. one less step ✅ this means that new strings will only go in when we branch, and with an sla of 3 days, they may be slightly delayed before the release... but that's not too bad. i think it's ok. plus, weekly releases, if a string doesn't make it, oh well, wait a week, it'll be there no problem. not the end of the world. possible improvement of that: release language packs when they change instead of on a schedule. that'll really be reactive... it's probably ok to do this, i think. ✅ [url] we still keep the same "[mirroring of vs code build steps]( [url] which isn't great but it's also what we had before so that's ok tracked: [url] |SEP| moving to 1.113 for verification to give localization some extra time to propagate the new strings.
solution_comments: ok so, given the importance of figuring this out quickly due to not getting new strings, i've made a short fix that doesn't make things worse and simplifies things a bit: we now run [this pipeline]( [url] daily, which will check for the latest [inline code] branch, if there's a newer one, it will do what it did before and update the repo to track that new branch. this will bump the engine version so everyone's happy. this means that endgame champ no longer has to run a pipeline to do this. one less step ✅ this means that new strings will only go in when we branch, and with an sla of 3 days, they may be slightly delayed before the release... but that's not too bad. i think it's ok. plus, weekly releases, if a string doesn't make it, oh well, wait a week, it'll be there no problem. not the end of the world. possible improvement of that: release language packs when they change instead of on a schedule. that'll really be reactive... it's probably ok to do this, i think. ✅ [url] we still keep the same "[mirroring of vs code build steps]( [url] which isn't great but it's also what we had before so that's ok tracked: [url]

clean_title: bug? integrated terminal font rendering appears thin / poorly antialiased in light themes on macos
clean_body: does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes/no type: bug platform: macos vs code version: 1.111.0 (observed in recent updates) environment: occurs across multiple macbook devices with different screen resolutions description in recent versions of vs code, the integrated terminal appears to have a font rendering issue when using light themes on macos . when both the editor theme and terminal theme are light , terminal fonts appear overly thin with visible pixel zig-zag artifacts , suggesting that font antialiasing is not being applied correctly. this significantly reduces readability. this behavior does not occur when: - using dark editor themes - using a light editor theme with a dark terminal theme the issue appears to affect all light themes tested , not just a specific theme. observed effects - terminal text appears very thin and jagged - antialiasing artifacts are visible - powerline / nerd fonts icons become difficult to distinguish - tui box-drawing characters become hard to read examples: - the frame around the “openai codex” banner in the screenshot becomes almost illegible. - similar rendering artifacts appear in other terminal-based tools that rely on text ui elements. ![image]( [url] ![image]( [url] steps to reproduce 1. use macos 2. install the latest version of vs code 3. select a light editor theme 4. use a light terminal theme 5. open the integrated terminal 6. use a terminal font such as nerd fonts / powerline fonts 7. run a tui application (example: codex cli) expected behavior terminal fonts should render with proper antialiasing and normal stroke thickness , consistent with how they appear: - in dark vs code themes - in native macos terminal or iterm - in vs code when using dark terminal themes troubleshooting attempted the following were tested but did not resolve the issue : - disabling macos system font antialiasing - enabling or disabling terminal font weight settings - setting the following in [inline code] : [code block] - testing across multiple macbook devices - testing across different display resolutions - testing with multiple light themes [code block]

all_comments_text: the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe. |SEP| hi -tree, thanks for reporting this. i've tried suggestion replacing the integer literals with dtype-matched constants, it's working fine in this [gist]( [url]
solution_comments: the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe.

all_comments_text: i've recently worked on constant folding for extract patches and would like to investigate this issue |SEP| hi -tree , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this issue. i ran your code on tensorflow 2.21.0-rc0 and the nightly version. while i encountered the same issues on 2.21.0-rc0, i observed that the problem has been resolved in the nightly build. therefore, this fix will be included in upcoming versions. i have attached a working colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| technical note on root cause and best practices it is great to see that the issue is mitigated in the tf 2.21 nightly build and that the compiler now handles this state without crashing. however, from an architectural standpoint, we should distinguish between a "crash fix" and a "performant implementation." the performance aspect: using python primitives like random.randint to determine shapes often forces the compiler into frequent re-tracing. each time the python value changes, xla may see it as a new graph signature, leading to unnecessary compilation overhead. recommendation for stability and speed: even with the latest fixes, it remains best practice to use native tensorflow semantics (e.g., tf.shape(x)[0]) instead of python-side generators. logical stability: ensures the graph is portable across cpu, gpu, and tpu backends. optimal performance: by using tf.shape, the dimensions remain symbolic within the same compiled executable. this avoids the "re-compilation trap" and allows xla to optimize the hardware kernels once for all input sizes. while the nightly fix prevents the failure, using tf.shape ensures the system remains lean and statically sound.

all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce this issue locally using the provided example. the root cause seems to be the use of tf.variable for a static configuration value (d model). when compiling with xla (jit compile=true), shape and configuration values need to be python primitives. replacing self.d model = tf.variable(d model) with self.d model = d model resolves the error completely on my end. i’ve opened a small documentation pr to clarify this usage so others don’t run into the same issue. |SEP| i was able to reproduce the same issue using tensorflow 2.21.0-rc0 as well as the nightly version. please find the [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi -tree thank you for your answer , yes, replacing [inline code] with [inline code] , will works fine.when xla tries to parse the variable as a static shape, it raises the error. i have attached a gist for your [reference]( [url]
solution_comments: i was able to reproduce this issue locally using the provided example. the root cause seems to be the use of tf.variable for a static configuration value (d model). when compiling with xla (jit compile=true), shape and configuration values need to be python primitives. replacing self.d model = tf.variable(d model) with self.d model = d model resolves the error completely on my end. i’ve opened a small documentation pr to clarify this usage so others don’t run into the same issue.

clean_title: a note on numerical preconditions in softmax gradient translation-invariance testing
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf2.3.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.7.12 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version cudnn 8.6 gpu model and memory tesla v100 current behavior? hello tensorflow team, we’d like to report and clarify an observation from metamorphic testing of operator gradients, focusing on how large constant shifts interact with the numerical preconditions of certain metamorphic relations (mrs). we tested three operators under the same “constant shift” stress condition: avgpool2d (ap) maxpool2d (mp) softmax we observed that avgpool2d consistently satisfies the expected mr, while maxpool2d and softmax can exhibit reproducible deviations under large shifts. we have already reported the maxpool2d case separately; this email summarizes the broader finding and asks for guidance on how best to interpret/document it, especially for softmax. background (the mrs we tested) 1) constant shift transformation ( x' = x + c ) where (c) is constant along the mr-relevant axis (e.g., per-window constant for pooling, per-softmax slice constant for softmax), and may vary across other dimensions via broadcasting. 2) expected behavior avgpool2d: forward shifts by a constant; backward should be invariant under the same upstream gradient: ( gx' ≈ gx ) maxpool2d (with the precondition that argmax routing is preserved): ( gx' ≈ gx ) softmax (theoretically shift-invariant along the normalization axis): ( \mathrm{softmax}(x + c) = \mathrm{softmax}(x) ) and, under the same upstream gradient (g), ( gx' ≈ gx ) what we observed avgpool2d under large constant shifts (including magnitudes on the order of 1e4–1e5 in float32), avgpool2d did not show violations in our metamorphic checks (forward/gradient consistency remained within tolerance). maxpool2d under settings such as padding=same and stride=1, we observe reproducible mr violations with large violation metrics (vr) on both cpu and gpu when (|c|) is sufficiently large (e.g., hundreds or larger). this maxpool2d finding has already been reported. [url] softmax under large constant shifts (e.g., 1e4–1e5 in float32) applied as a constant along the softmax axis (via broadcasting), we observe reproducible numerical deviations in: forward output consistency and/or backward gradient consistency (gx) the effect appears implementation-path sensitive (e.g., cpu vs gpu and/or layout paths), though we can provide exact version/platform matrices and minimal repro scripts. our analysis / hypothesis we believe the maxpool2d behavior is expected under finite precision and ties: for large (|c|), float32 ulp becomes coarse relative to small differences. previously strictly ordered values can become equal or nearly equal after the shift (implicit ties). this can change argmax routing, hence backward routing changes and the mr precondition (“order preserved”) no longer holds. for softmax, we suspect a related but distinct numerical-stability / implementation-path-consistency issue: softmax is mathematically shift-invariant along the normalization axis, but under extreme magnitudes in float32, different kernel paths / reduction orders / stabilization behaviors can potentially lead to observable deviations. the fact that avgpool2d passes under the same stress condition suggests the phenomenon is not a general issue in the test harness, but operator-specific numerical behavior. questions we’d like to confirm the following points: for maxpool2d gradients, is it reasonable to treat violations under large constant shifts as not applicable (n/a) when the “argmax routing preserved” precondition is broken by finite-precision tie effects? for softmax, is it expected that extremely large constant shifts (float32) may lead to measurable forward/gradient deviations across device/layout implementations, or would you consider this worthy of investigation as an implementation-path consistency / numerical stability issue? would it be helpful to add a brief documentation note stating that large constant shifts can affect: maxpool gradient routing (due to finite-precision tie effects), and potentially softmax numerical consistency in extreme regimes, even though the underlying mathematical properties hold in real arithmetic? reproduction we have minimal deterministic scripts and concrete configurations (tf version, cpu/gpu, layout) for: maxpool2d (already reported), and softmax (new observation) we can provide them in this thread immediately if useful. thank you for your time and for the continued work on tensorflow. best regards, xiaohuaovo standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

clean_title: tensorflow 2.20.0 causes mutex crash in huggingface tokenizers on macos arm without being imported
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source binary tensorflow version 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution macos (apple silicon / arm64) - mac studio tahoe 26.1 mobile device no response python version 3.13.11 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.4.4.1) cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory apple silicon (mps backend) m2 ultra current behavior? simply having tensorflow 2.20.0 installed in a python environment causes a fatal mutex crash when using huggingface tokenizers, even though tensorflow is never imported in the code. when running code that loads a diffusers fluxpipeline (which uses huggingface tokenizers internally), the process crashes with: libc++abi: terminating due to uncaught exception of type std:: 1::system error: mutex lock failed: invalid argument this happens at the tokenizer loading stage, specifically when t5tokenizerfast is being initialized. expected behavior: tensorflow being installed but not imported should have no effect on unrelated libraries. the huggingface tokenizers library should load normally. key findings from debugging: 1. with tensorflow installed → crash occurs (tensorflow is never imported) 2. uninstall only tensorflow → works immediately 3. all tensorflow dependencies (keras, tensorboard, grpcio, h5py, etc.) can remain installed without issue 4. the issue is specifically the tensorflow package itself, not its dependencies 5. downgrading protobuf does not fix the issue 6. setting tokenizers parallelism=false does not fix the issue this suggests tensorflow 2.20.0 may be registering something at the native/shared library level that interferes with the rust-based tokenizers library's threading primitives on macos arm. standalone code to reproduce the issue [inline code] fluxpipeline [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] pip install torch torchvision diffusers transformers accelerate safetensors sentencepiece protobuf tensorflow 2. run the following python script: import torch from diffusers import fluxpipeline crash occurs during this call pipe = fluxpipeline.from pretrained( "black-forest-labs/flux.1-dev", torch dtype=torch.float16 ) pipe.to("mps") log output /users/username/venv-metal/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/amp/autocast mode.py:270: userwarning: user provided device type of 'cuda', but cuda is not available. disabling warnings.warn( loading pipeline components...: 0%| | 0/7 [00:00

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for reporting the issue. the crash is caused by invalid inputs passed to [inline code] , rather than a problem in the split gpu kernel itself. i noticed two issues in your code: - axis is created using [inline code] , which produces a scalar tensor with value 1. for split, the axis must be a python integer or a scalar tensor containing the intended axis value. example of valid usage: [code block] - the input tensor has shape [73271, 29308], which is extremely large and requires about 8.6 gb of gpu memory. split then produces two outputs plus additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement exceeds 15 gb, which is beyond typical gpu limits. because of these invalid inputs, the gpu kernel receives incorrect offsets and attempts to read outside the allocated buffer, resulting in the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when valid axis values and reasonable tensor sizes are used, the op works correctly. i am attaching a [gist]( [url] for your reference. i hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

all_comments_text: i've noticed that in the methods executorstate::processsync, gpudevice::compute, and opkernel::compute (tensorflow v2.5), there does not appear to be explicit cuda stream synchronization. my concern is that if an operation's cuda kernel has not finished executing before the input tensor is deallocated, it could result in access to freed memory or race conditions. |SEP| hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here we appreciate your detailed observation. you are right that cuda kernels execute asynchronously, and premature tensor deallocation could theoretically lead to race conditions or use-after-free issues. however, tensorflow manages this safely without [inline code] calls in functions such as [inline code] , [inline code] , or [inline code] . instead, tensorflow uses an event-based deferred deallocation mechanism implemented in the gpu runtime. this design ensures that memory used by a cuda kernel is not freed until the kernel’s execution has completed. here are a few references that explain this behavior: - [tensorflow gpu event manager source (gpu event mgr.cc)]( [url] handles deferred frees and event polling. - [tensorflow gpu guide]( [url] describes asynchronous gpu memory behavior and allocator configuration. - [nvidia tensorflow user guide (pdf)]( [url] documents the tf gpu allocator=cuda malloc async option for asynchronous allocation and freeing. so even though there are no explicit synchronization calls in those executor methods, tensorflow ensures correctness through deferred-free and event-driven synchronization mechanisms in the gpu runtime. i hope this clarifies your concern. thank you!

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this issue. the behavior you observed is expected [inline code] and [inline code] instancenorm2d are conceptually different. according to the [official keras documentation]( [url] [inline code] normalizes activations across the batch (subtracting the batch mean and dividing by the batch standard deviation). it aggregates statistics across the batch and does not normalize each sample individually. in contrast, pytorch’s [inline code] normalizes each instance independently across spatial dimensions for each channel. that’s why in your example: [code block] the output mean remains nearly the same batchnorm aggregates batch-wide statistics, so similar samples share nearly identical normalization. to replicate [inline code] behavior in tensorflow, you can use tf.keras.layers.groupnormalization with groups=channels, which normalizes each channel independently. here is the [gist]( [url] for your reference. this achieves the same normalization behavior as pytorch’s instancenorm2d(channels). you can also refer to the [groupnormalization documentation]( [url] for more details. thank you!

clean_title: tensorflow lite reference kernel reducesum overflows when network type not uint8
clean_body: system information - linux ubuntu 22.04 - tensorflow installed from pip source - tensorflow version 2.19.1 - tflite runtime installed from pip source - tflite runtime version 2.14.0 standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] the example network can be created from the attached file minimal.json. the network contains a single reducesum op, the input tensor has shape and type int8[1,128]. output is [[-1]] when it should be [[127]] [minimal.json]( [url] any other info / logs i have located the bug in the tensorflow lite reference kernel, in file /tensorflow/lite/kernels/reduce.cc. in the function quantizedmeanorsum, when kernel type is kreference, the call to reference ops::quantizedmeanorsum uses type uint8 instead of template type t. this results in overflow when a network has a different type. i have included the bug fix as a git patch below. [code block]

all_comments_text: hi , thank you for raising this issue. the root cause appears to be that the current tensorflow pip binaries do not include precompiled cuda kernels for the nvidia blackwell architecture (compute capability 12.0, used in the rtx 50 series). as a result, when tensorflow falls back to jit compilation from ptx, the generated ptx code is rejected by the cuda 13.x driver, leading to [inline code] and [inline code] errors. this is a known and ongoing issue. please refer to the following tracking [issue]( [url] for further updates and technical discussions. some of the comments there may also be helpful for your setup. thank you! |SEP| i can confirm encountering this exact issue on an nvidia geforce rtx 5080 gpu using tensorflow 2.16+. the issue seems indeed related to the lack of precompiled kernels for compute capability 12.0 (blackwell) and xla compilation failures with the new drivers. however, i managed to get a stable training loop and inference running by explicitly disabling xla devices and jit compilation. workaround that worked for me: 1. disable xla devices (must be done before importing tensorflow): [code block] 2. disable jit in keras models: when compiling your model, explicitly set [inline code] : [code block] with these settings, i am able to utilize the gpu for training without the [inline code] crash. |SEP| hi, i think that this could help: [url]

all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce the same issue using tensorflow versions 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. i am providing a [gist]( [url] here for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi , this is intended behavior for xla. the xla compiler is optimized for numerical computations (linear algebra) and does not natively support [inline code] types or operations like [inline code] that rely on hash tables and variable-length data. when you use [inline code] , you force the entire function context to run on the xla device. since the function input is a string tensor, xla attempts to create an entry point ( [inline code] ) for a string, which fails because xla kernels only support numerical types (floats, ints, bools). solution: you must separate the preprocessing (strings - ints) from the computation (ints - output). the preprocessing should run in standard tensorflow (cpu), and the resulting integers can then be passed to the jit-compiled function. here is the corrected architectural pattern: python import tensorflow as tf from tensorflow import keras from tensorflow.keras import layers class testmodel(keras.model): def init (self): super(). init () 1. preprocessing (cpu/standard runtime) self.number encoding = layers.stringlookup(vocabulary=["1", "2", "3", "4", "5"]) 2. computation (xla compatible) self.embedding = layers.embedding(input dim=self.number encoding.vocabulary size(), output dim=64) def call(self, inputs): step a: convert strings to ints (not compiled) encoded = self.number encoding(inputs) step b: run math on xla (compiled) return self.compiled embedding(encoded) .function(jit compile=true) def compiled embedding(self, encoded inputs): this function only sees integers, so xla is good return self.embedding(encoded inputs) inputs = tf.constant(["3", "1", "4", "2", "5"]) model = testmodel() output = model(inputs) print("success:", output.shape) |SEP| hi , thanks for your patience. this is expected behavior. xla only supports numerical tensor types, so [inline code] inputs are not compatible when [inline code] is enabled. all inputs to a jit-compiled function need to be xla-supported types. the usual pattern is to keep preprocessing outside the jit-compiled function. after moving stringlookup out of the compiled function, the example runs correctly under xla. i have shared a minimal working [gist]( [url] for reference. -codes thanks for the helpful explanation.

all_comments_text: hi, this issue occurs because keras.layers.lstm with default parameters uses the cudnn fast path, which is not supported by xla gpu jit. a simple workaround is to disable the cudnn path by setting recurrent dropout 0. for example: layers.lstm(32, recurrent dropout=0.1, return sequences=false) this allows the lstm to compile and run under xla on gpu while still functioning correctly. this works in my ubuntu 24.04 [issue 100872.py]( [url] |SEP| hi -jain7, thanks for your pointers and quick response. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. i was able to reproduce the same behavior with tensorflow 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. as -jain7 mentioned, setting recurrent dropout 0 disables the cudnn fast path and allows the lstm to compile under xla gpu jit. i tried this workaround and it works fine for me as well. here is a minimal [gist]( [url] with the workaround for reference. thank you!

[inline code] if you want to remove these warnings and use cpu only, you can install the cpu-only package with the following command: [code block] after this, importing tensorflow will show only minimal informational logs about cpu optimizations, and all gpu-related warnings will disappear. i tried this myself, and the warnings were gone. i am attaching a screenshot for your reference. thank you!

all_comments_text: from the linker documentation the –b symbolic(functions) options are intended for specialized dynamic objects and are not recommended for general use. although these options can reduce runtime relocation overhead, they can compromise bindings that expect interposition. to reduce runtime relocation processing, the creation of a version definition or use of direct bindings is recommended over using these options. in other words it is an optimization technique to shorten relocation times. framework.so is by design supposed to be imported by many other libraries (all tf's own python bindings and many other libs which depend on it). yes, it does not work well, but that is a different discussion, and a decision which had been made many years ago, so we are stuck with it. main point of this issue is the need to control and limit exported symbols by providing version script to framework.so.2, though matching pr ( [url] introduces version script which still exports [everything ]( [url] yes, the general idea of properly controlling exported symbols is what we would like to have, the bigger [inline code] has it (but still exports tons of stuff), [inline code] by design is a small subset of tf, that can be exported by anyone, thus its entire body is exported. in other words, the version script part did not change the behavior, the symbolic-functions arg broke mac build, and i don't think the symbolic optimization technique is appropriate for a binary which is being loaded all over the place (both automatically via dynamic section resolution and often manually by [inline code] in downstream projects), as it is likely to brake downstream projects in weird ways. |SEP| -google i'm not sure how [url] could break mac build since all options shouldn't downstream projects), as it is likely to brake downstream projects in weird ways. this pr was aimed at fixing tf already breaking in weird ways due to symbol interposition (which the symbolic flag is clean_title: why does register replacement not support fuzzy matching for optional parameters in operators when using search fn or search fn pattern?
clean_body: i am attempting to write a pattern matcher to match fused operators, and currently encounter an issue: neither search fn nor search fn pattern provides a way to perform fuzzy matching for optional parameters. for example, optional parameters like offset and bias in the following operator prototype appear differently in the fx graph when they are passed or omitted: operator prototype as follows: [code block] case 1 (with optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1, offset=arg5 1, bias=arg6 1); case 2 (without optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1); attempt 1: using search fn the following search fn only matches case 1 and fails to match case 2 (where optional parameters are omitted). to cover case 2, i have to write a separate search fn: [code block] attempt 2: using search fn pattern (callfunction) i also tried using search fn pattern (from torch. inductor.pattern matcher import register replacement as register) and wrote a callfunction as shown below. however, using ignored() results in a placeholder , which causes a failure during the matching process when verifying len(self.args)!=len(node.args). it seems impossible to match both cases with a single callfunction: [code block] impact: some operators may have more optional parameters (e.g., n optional parameters). in such cases, i would need to write 2ⁿ search fn or search fn pattern to cover all scenarios, which is extremely inefficient and unscalable. questions: 1. is there any syntax to perform fuzzy matching for this scenario, so that both cases (with/without optional parameters passed) can be matched with a single pattern? 2. what is the difference between the two writing methods in the matching process: 1.callfunction(offset=keywordarg('offset')) 2.callfunction(keywordarg('offset'))? cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: torch.func transforms silently return zeros under inference mode
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] silently produce incorrect results (zeros) when called under [inline code] . no error is raised. [code block] root cause functorch transforms set up their own autograd context on entry: enable grad() for grad/vjp, set fwd grad enabled(true) for jvp (see note [grad and vjp interaction with no grad]). however, inference mode also excludes autograd dispatch keys from the tls dispatch key set, and the mode-enabling calls don't undo that. so autograd dispatch keys remain excluded and differentiation silently produces zeros. the transforms already correctly handle no grad — this is the same class of issue with a different tls mechanism. expected behavior either produce correct results (by also disabling inference mode on transform entry, matching the existing no grad handling) or raise an error. cc
clean_text: torch.func transforms silently return zeros under inference mode 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] silently produce incorrect results (zeros) when called under [inline code] . no error is raised. all_comments_text: hi, i'd like to work on this issue. my proposed approach is to raise an explicit error when torch.func.{grad, vjp, jvp} are called under torch.inference mode(), rather than silently returning incorrect zero results. rationale: - inference mode() is explicitly designed for non-differentiable inference workloads (with autograd disabled), so it's inconsistent to allow gradient-related transforms like grad/vjp/jvp to run in this mode. - an explicit error is far safer than silent failure (which can lead to hard-to-debug issues for users), aligning with pytorch's philosophy of "fail fast" for invalid usage. i'm happy to adjust the approach (e.g., temporarily disable inference mode to return correct results instead) if the maintainers prefer that direction. |SEP| hi and , i'm hoping that we can get this to work rather than error. i've proposed changes [here]( [url] and a use case: molecular dynamics (but any sensitivity analysis or ode use case could benefit). thanks! |SEP| hi and , i totally understand that it would be convenient for use cases like molecular dynamics, sensitivity analysis, and ode solvers if these function transforms could work under torch.inference mode(). however, i believe we should raise an explicit error instead of silently returning incorrect zeros or trying to make them work. torch.inference mode() is explicitly designed for inference-only workloads with autograd fully disabled. running gradient-related transforms like grad, vjp, and jvp inside this mode is semantically inconsistent. allowing them to run silently not only produces wrong results but also violates the design contract of inference mode. for users who need differentiation while avoiding unnecessary gradient tracking, torch.no grad() is already the appropriate choice. therefore, i think the correct fix is to detect when these apis are used under inference mode() and raise a clear error message to prevent silent failures. |SEP| it clean_title: cpu inductor backward crash: heap corruption with fancy indexing + einsum
clean_body: [inline code] on cpu produces a backward pass that crashes with heap corruption (segfault, [inline code] , [inline code] ) when the forward graph combines fancy indexing with an einsum clean_title: streamcontextvariable's create proxy logic removed in dynamo post-2.9, breaking custom backends relying on set stream nodes
clean_body: i observed that in pytorch 2.10, the enter and exit methods of streamcontextvariablein the dynamo component have removed the logic for creating proxies via tx.output.create proxy. in contrast, pytorch 2.9 retains this logic: [code block] this change causes the set stream nodes to disappear from fx graphs in pytorch 2.10+, whereas they were present in 2.9. related pr maybe: [url] example [code block] in torch2.9, fx graph will be: [code block] impact:​ my custom torch.compilebackend relies on these set stream nodes in the fx graph to perform stream-related operations before aotautograd. the removal of these nodes breaks my workflow, as the necessary stream configuration is no longer captured in the graph. questions: what motivated the removal of create proxyin streamcontextvariable? are there alternative approaches to retain the original behavior (e.g., explicit stream management) in newer dynamo versions? cc -chen -nrv

clean_body: bug [inline code] defines: [code block] the script uses [inline code] to generate kernel dispatch ranges: - [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] since [inline code] is the last element , no [inline code] pair is created. this means no cutlass mem eff attention kernels are generated for sm 100+ architectures . blackwell gpus (sm 120/121, [inline code] 1200/1201) have no kernel dispatch coverage. the generated guard clauses and dispatch functions simply never match blackwell's compute capability, so [inline code] has no kernels to dispatch to on these gpus. root cause missing upper bound in the [inline code] list at line 25 of [inline code] . the [inline code] approach requires a sentinel value beyond the last real architecture to create the final dispatch range. suggested fix [code block] adding [inline code] as a sentinel creates the pair [inline code] , which generates sm100 kernels covering [inline code] 1000–1999 — including blackwell at 1200/1201. sm100 (hopper) kernels are forward-compatible with sm120 (blackwell). after changing the sm list, regenerate kernels: [code block] this updates the generated [inline code] and [inline code] files with the correct dispatch ranges. patch [code block] environment - gpu : nvidia dgx spark — blackwell gb10 (sm 12.1) - cuda : 13.0 - architecture : aarch64 - pytorch : main branch @ [inline code] verification after applying the fix and rebuilding: - [inline code] returns [inline code] - gpu tensor allocation + [inline code] passes - build completes without errors related issues - — add official support for cuda sm 120 (blackwell) - — official support for sm 120 in stable pytorch builds - — auto-detected arch flags omit accelerated suffix on blackwell cc /cuda

all_comments_text: i would like to work on this issue. could you please assign it to me? i am investigating why the results differ between default and channels last memory formats. |SEP| this is likely to be expected behavior since kaiming uniform and uniform generate random numbers by iterating over the underlying storage. if the memory layout differs, the same random values get mapped to different logical positions in the tensor. a workaround would be to - create the model in regular format - initialize it - then convert the model to be channels last |SEP| i have implemented the native fix for this inconsistency by ensuring initialization happens on a contiguous buffer, as discussed. i've also added a regression test (linked above) that verifies bit-wise parity across memory formats. the pr is now ready for a final review.
solution_comments: this is likely to be expected behavior since kaiming uniform and uniform generate random numbers by iterating over the underlying storage. if the memory layout differs, the same random values get mapped to different logical positions in the tensor. a workaround would be to - create the model in regular format - initialize it - then convert the model to be channels last |SEP| i have implemented the native fix for this inconsistency by ensuring initialization happens on a contiguous buffer, as discussed. i've also added a regression test (linked above) that verifies bit-wise parity across memory formats. the pr is now ready for a final review.

clean_title: [torch.compile] massive numerical discrepancy ( 0.05 max abs error) in conv+bn+silu fusion with inductor fallback random=1
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using torch.compile with the inductor backend and enabling inductor fallback random=1, there is a significant numerical divergence compared to eager mode. in a standalone reproduction involving the first three blocks of a yolov8-like architecture (conv + bn + silu), the max absolute error reaches 0.05, and the max relative error exceeds 10^5. this suggests an unstable numerical approximation within the fused kernels. observed results: comparing mode a (eager execution) vs mode b (compiled with inductor fallback random=1): max absolute error: 5.4588e-02 max relative error: 1.0962e+05 rel l2 error (global): 3.8443e-04 eager output range: [-0.2785, 97.1013] compiled output range: [-0.2785, 97.0889] why this matters: while a 0.05 absolute error might seem small in isolation, this discrepancy is roughly 4 orders of magnitude higher than the expected numerical noise for fp32 fusion (typically < 1e-6). in full-scale yolov8 models, i have observed this error accumulating across layers, eventually reaching a max absolute error of 0.4 to 0.6, which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability. reproduction script: this script is truly standalone and does not require any external weights or data files. it manually initializes the weights to ensure intermediate activations reach a magnitude that exposes the numerical instability. all_comments_text: [physics reject] the $0.05$ maximum absolute error is strictly working as intended; enabling compiler optimization (inductor fallback random=1) implicitly triggers batch normalization weight folding, which, when executed via the default tensorfloat-32 (tf32) kernels on ampere architecture (rtx 3090), aggressively amplifies the inherent 10-bit mantissa truncation during fused mac operations, meaning this discrepancy is mathematically bounded by the hardware's low-precision limits rather than a compiler bug. |SEP| inductor fallback random=0 gives the same result, so this is likely not related to rng handling. the root cause is [inline code] defaults to true and tf32 inherently has fewer mantissa bits than fp32 causing precision error to accumulate across multiple conv layers. which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability i'd recommend applying torch.compile to both the training and inference of the model, to ensure numerical behavior stays the same and reduce the chance of model reliability issue.

clean_title: o(n^2) in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass
clean_body: there is a o(n^2) loop in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass, which dominates export time of large models, including most huggingface llms. (n: number of initializers) the quadraticity comes from all-to-all comparison between all initializers: [url] since 1) [inline code] is called once for each of n initializers, and 2) [inline code] is a vector of initializers, this loop is o(n^2). although [inline code] function often only compares tensor metadata (shape, dtype, etc) and not actually compare the values of the tensors if the metadata didn't match, this loop starts to dominates the export time of large models with many initializers. in my observation, 1k initializers seems to be the tipping point, which is the case for most llms. reproducible example [code block] output [code block] suggestion define [inline code] as [inline code] instead of [inline code] with a proper hash function. with this, the deduplication pass can be significantly optimized, possibly into o(n). these are the possible hash keys for hashing [inline code] : tensor metadata -- shape, dtype, strides, ... rationale: this will reduce the export time by a significant constant factor. however, the time complexity remains o(n^2) if the number of tensors with same shape and dtype increases with n (which is true in llms) first element of the tensor rationale: most tensors differ from one another from the very first element. with a practical assumption that $p(x 1 = y 1 \rightarrow x = y) \approx 1$ , this will optimize the deduplication pass into o(n) in most practical cases. cc -nrv

all_comments_text: the precision loss occurs because the padding parameter is strictly typed as float in the aten dispatcher definition (mapping to c++ double), leading to irreversible mantissa truncation for 64-bit integers exceeding 53 bits before being passed to the tensor fill operation. [code block] [code block] |SEP| i was able to reproduce this on google colab (python 3.12, pytorch 2.10.0+cpu). reproduction script: pythonimport torch from torch import tensor from torch.nested import nested tensor sample = nested tensor([ tensor([1, - [hash] ]), tensor([- [hash] ]) ]) padded = torch.nested.to padded tensor(sample, padding=- [hash] ) print(padded) expected = - [hash] actual = padded[1][1].item() print(f"expected: {expected}") print(f"got: {actual}") print(f"precision lost: {expected != actual}") output: tensor([[ 1, - [hash] ], [- [hash] , - [hash] ]]) expected: - [hash] got: - [hash] precision lost: true this confirms the bug. the discrepancy of 112 is consistent with the float64 mantissa truncation described by -logic — 64-bit integers beyond 53 bits of precision are silently corrupted when the padding value is cast through a c++ double. |SEP| can you help triage this? |SEP| as -logic mentions the number is not representable as cpp double, so not much we can do here

all_comments_text: [physics reject] the $10^{-3}$ discrepancy is working as intended; enabling inductor shape padding=1 forces memory alignment for tensor core utilization, inherently triggering implicit tensorfloat-32 (tf32) low-precision matrix kernels on ampere gpus, meaning the observed error is strictly bounded by the mathematical limits of the tf32 10-bit mantissa. |SEP| -logic for users who prioritize consistency over peak throughput, please provide an implementation that avoids inf - inf (nan) scenarios in fused kernels, ensuring predictable behavior even under extreme values. 2. consistent error exposure: our core request is to ensure that numerical anomalies are exposed in a consistent form across different backends, rather than manifesting as completely different error states (e.g., nan on gpu vs. masked "valid" values on cpu). |SEP| bitwise equivalence is not yet expected here. closing. |SEP| hi -logic i understand that bitwise equivalence is not a current goal, but there is a significant usability issue here. the core problem is that inductor shape padding is marketed as a memory alignment optimization, yet it implicitly toggles precision-altering hardware paths (tf32) without the user's explicit consent. if a user has already set torch.set float32 matmul precision('highest'), they expect fp32 precision. if shape padding=1 overrides this global precision setting to enable tensor cores, it violates the principle of least astonishment. at the very least, could this side-effect be documented, or should inductor respect the global matmul precision setting when deciding whether to pad for tensor cores? closing this as "intended" ignores the fact that users are losing 13 bits of precision silently.

clean_title: numerical discrepancy in nn.embedding between eager and compile modes
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using torch.compile() on nn.embedding layer, the output differs from eager mode even with identical device (cuda) and dtype (float32). the relative l2 error is 2.0980e-04, which exceeds the 1e-4 threshold. key observations: - same device (cuda) and dtype (float32), only optimization mode differs - weight range: [-0.0920, 0.0921] (normal, no extreme values) - all outputs are valid (no nan/inf) - this discrepancy could accumulate in deep models, potentially affecting downstream predictions expected behavior: eager and compile modes should produce identical (or within acceptable tolerance) numerical results for the same inputs. actual behavior: compile mode produces relative l2 error of 2.0980e-04 compared to eager mode. replicate tensor in placement types.py 1. torch.chunk uses ceiling division: local size = (s + n - 1) // n 2. all gather concatenates n chunks: gathered = n ((s + n - 1) // n) 3. maybe unpad tensor clean_title: [release 2.11][vllm] distributed failures - free memory on device cuda:0 (19.77/22.05 gib) on startup is less than desired gpu memory utilization (0.9, 19.84 gib)
clean_body: 24.02.2026: [url] analysis: title: pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0 increases baseline gpu memory usage, breaking memory-tight workloads environment: - pytorch 2.11.0+cu130 - cuda 13.0 / driver 570.133.20 - nvidia l4 (22.05 gib) - ray distributed executor - vllm (pipeline parallel test) description: after upgrading to pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0, the baseline gpu memory consumed before any model loading has increased compared to previous pytorch/cuda versions. on an nvidia l4 (22.05 gib total), only 19.77 gib is free after cuda context initialization, whereas previously enough memory was available for a 90% utilization target (19.84 gib). the 70 mib increase in baseline memory usage causes workloads that target gpu memory utilization=0.9 to fail with an oom-style error during device initialization, before any model weights are loaded. this occurs in a ray distributed setup (2 gpus, pp=2) running microsoft/phi-3.5-moe-instruct with dummy weights. the same configuration worked on prior pytorch + cuda versions. expected behavior: baseline cuda context memory consumption should not increase significantly between pytorch releases, or at minimum should be documented as a known change. reproduction: initialize cuda on an l4 gpu with pytorch 2.11+cu130 via ray workers and check torch.cuda.mem get info() — free memory is lower than with pytorch 2.10 or earlier. [code block] cc

clean_title: masked scatter flattens the source array on mps (apple silicon)
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug code behavior is different between mps and cpu/cuda. the bug is only relevant to mps device (apple silicon). tensor.masked scatter (mask, source) copies elements from source into self tensor at positions where the mask is true. elements from source are copied into self starting at position 0 of source and continuing in order one-by-one for each occurrence of mask being true. the shape of mask must be [broadcastable]( [url] with the shape of the underlying tensor. the source should have at least as many elements as the number of ones in mask. a minimal example to reproduce a bug: [code block] expected behavior: the behavior of 'masked scatter' is consistent across different accelerator devices. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: none rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: macos 26.3 (arm64) gcc version: could not collect clang version: 17.0.0 (clang-1700.6.3.2) cmake version: version 4.2.3 libc version: n/a python version: 3.10.19 (main, feb 4 2026, 00:02:50) [clang 21.1.4 ] (64-bit runtime) python platform: macos-26.3-arm64-arm-64bit is cuda available: false cuda runtime version: no cuda cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: no cuda nvidia driver version: no cuda cudnn version: no cuda is xpu available: false hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: true caching allocator config: n/a cpu: apple m4 pro versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] numpy==2.2.6 [pip3] torch==2.7.0 [pip3] torchaudio==2.7.0 [pip3] torchmetrics==1.8.2 [pip3] torchvision==0.22.0 [conda] could not collect cc

clean_body: the test [inline code] in [inline code] fails with [xpass(strict)] on cuda because of the xfail. test class : inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py::testgpuwrapper::test mm plus mm3 gpu wrapper platform : cuda error : xpass(strict) - test was expected to fail but passes steps to reproduce 1. build pytorch with cuda support. 2. run: [inline code] 4. observe [inline code] failing with [xpass(strict)] because it passes but is marked as expected to fail. root cause pr [ ]( [url] added an xfail on [inline code] in [inline code] with the reason "c++ wrapper dynamic shapes fails on cuda, fixed on rocm." on cuda, the test [inline code] actually passes, so it fails with [inline code] error. only the dynamic shapes variant [inline code] fails on cuda. so the xfail is too broad: it marks both tests as expected to fail when only the dynamic shapes run fails. proposed fix: remove the xfail from [inline code] and skip the dynamic shapes variant on cuda in [inline code] (similar to test mm plus mm2 dynamic shapes). so that the [inline code] test runs and passes on cuda. the dynamic shapes test is skipped on cuda (where it fails) and runs on rocm (where it passes). hence, for [inline code] , if we remove the xfail on the test and use a skip in [inline code] the dynamic variant which is known to fail on cuda is skipped and the xpass issue will be resolved. i have a fix for this and if this is a good approach i can go ahead with this. code references base test : test/inductor/test select algorithm.py:299 (test mm plus mm3) gpu wrapper config : test/inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py:107-125 (test failures gpu wrapper) cc: -xq -turnansky cc -chen -nrv

all_comments_text: after reading only the onnx operation spec, i don't quite understand the reason for the dynamic shape constraint. is this a constraint of the torch-exported program? yes. [url] you can use [inline code] parameter: dynamic shapes ([dict]( [url] any] | [tuple]( [url] ...] | [list]( [url] | none) – a dictionary or a tuple of dynamic shapes for the model inputs. refer to [torch.export.export()]( [url] for more details. this is only used (and preferred) when dynamo is true. note that dynamic shapes is designed to be used when the model is exported with dynamo=true, while dynamic axes is used when dynamo=false. |SEP| after looking into this a bit more, i found that while the affine grid/grid sample onnx opset exists, it is not implemented in the cuda onnx runtime.

clean_title: [release 2.11][vllm] quantization - test pre quantized model, torchao
clean_body: ci: [url] - 19.02.2026 test pre quantized model summary: the error: assert config.version == 2, f"unexpected version: {config.version}" assertionerror: unexpected version: 1 the model drisspg/fp8-opt-125m was quantized with an older torchao that used float8 config version 1, but the torchao version in your torch 2.11 image expects version 2. this is a breaking change in torchao's float8 dynamic activation float8 weight quantize tensor. the flow is: 1. torchaoconfig.from config detects is checkpoint torchao serialized = true (the model has quant method: torchao) 2. during create weights, it calls torchao quantize param data → quantize () to pre-quantize weights 3. torchao's quantize () hits the version assertion because the model's config is v1 this isn't something fixable in vllm — the model checkpoint itself is incompatible with the newer torchao. the options are: 1. re-quantize the model with the new torchao and push an updated drisspg/fp8-opt-125m 2. skip the test for this torch version (if this is a ci concern) 3. use a different fp8 test model that was quantized with the newer torchao [code block] cc

clean_title: ub in gemmhelper: vector::reserve() followed by operator[] access
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] in [inline code] does: [code block] reserve(1) allocates capacity but keeps size() at 0. accessing [0] is undefined behavior. with libc++ hardening ( libcpp hardening mode extensive), operator[] includes bounds checking and calls builtin trap() on violation, producing sigtrap at runtime. fix: reserve(1) → resize(1). the same pattern existed in aten/src/aten/native/mps/operations/shape.mm (inputtensors .reserve) but was incidentally eliminated by the refactor in . versions found by code inspection. present in current main (as of a79cc95) and all releases through v2.10.0. cc -arm

all_comments_text: thanks for your proposal. could you elaborate more on why .detach() does not work? |SEP| you can already do something like [code block] why do you need a new api? |SEP| thank you for the suggestion! really appreciate you jumping in with a concrete code example. unfortunately this pattern doesn't solve the exact problem described in the issue (and the one i hit in practice). why it doesn't work for this use-case 1. it bypasses the shared part after the cut point in the real architecture there is a non-trivial module between [inline code] and the heads: [code block] python def forward with cut grad(self, x): return self.forward(x), self.forward(x.detach()) having this above function working without doing duplicate compute would be an equivalent solution. i encounter this problem a lot. for example i have one module that compresses text into a single vector and another module that takes a compressed vector and returns vectors that represent lower level text/tokens. in such cases there are losses that are safe to backprop all the way onto the text encoder and some that bring the network to a mode collapse. additionally, this is a common situation with adversarial networks. please reopen and/or consider finding any other solution for this. i'm using the wasteful forward with cut grad version (as calling retain graph costs me too much memory)

clean_title: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group
clean_body: hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang
clean_text: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang
all_comments_text: this feels like a [url] topic. imo what is missing in your proposal, is why such thing is needed. this is very technically challenging problem(and significantly impact teams ability to innovate), and i can't imagine a real world scenario where something like that would be necessary/beneficial. if one plans to spawn a distributed training, they should be able to install the same version of software on all node, shouldn't they? |SEP| thanks for the thoughtful feedback, — that makes sense. you're absolutely right that homogeneous deployments are the expected and recommended model. the intent here is not to support long-term mixed-version clusters. the motivation is more about failure diagnostics than compatibility. in practice, accidental version skew can happen during: rolling upgrades in long-running clusters ci or staging environments where cached environments differ across nodes shared research clusters where jobs inherit different module environments in those cases, the resulting failure mode is often: silent hang in collective ops low-level nccl/gloo errors mysterious mismatches that are difficult to trace back to version differences the proposal is not to enable version interoperability, but simply to detect incompatible protocol revisions early during init process group() and produce a clear error message. if the position is that mixed-version deployments are unsupported and we are comfortable with current failure modes, that's completely reasonable. i mainly wanted to clarify whether: this scenario is already guarded implicitly somewhere, or there would be interest in improving the diagnostic experience for accidental skew. if this discussion would be better suited for dev-discuss, i'm happy to move it there. |SEP| i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable. |SEP| thanks and for the thoughtful feedback — this is very helpful. the maintenance burden and protocol boundary concerns make sense. if version tracking isn't consistently updated or clearly scoped across communication layers, it could create misleading guarantees rather than improve reliability. given that, and the expectation of homogeneous deployments, i agree this is probably not actionable in its current form. appreciate you both taking the time to discuss this.
solution_comments: i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable.

no kernel image | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] (cuda tensors) | fail | | [inline code] | fail - cublas status execution failed | cuobjdump confirms sm 120 cubins exist: [code block] cublas64 12.dll also contains sm 120 cubins. versions tested all fail identically: - [inline code] (stable) - [inline code] (stable) - [inline code] (stable) nvidia drivers tested both fail identically: - driver 576.52 (cuda 12.9) - driver 591.86 (cuda 13.1) environment - gpu: nvidia geforce rtx 5060 8gb (pny dual fan, desktop) - os: windows 11 (build 26200.7705) - cpu: intel core i7-14700 - python: 3.12.10 - cuda toolkit: 12.8 installed locally (not required by pytorch but tested) - cupy: 13.6.0 with cupy-cuda12x — works perfectly on same gpu reproduction steps [code block] key insight the cubins are present in the dll but fail to load/execute . this is not a missing kernel issue — it appears to be a cubin format or loading issue specific to windows builds. runtime-compiled kernels (via cupy/nvrtc) work perfectly, confirming the gpu hardware and cuda driver stack are functional. impact this completely blocks gpu acceleration for applications using pytorch on rtx 5060 on windows (e.g., setiastro suite pro for astrophotography image processing). users who purchased rtx 5060 gpus specifically for pytorch-based gpu compute cannot use them at all. related issues - clean_title: avoid cuda sync in spectral decompositions
clean_body: 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch current docs of spectral decompositions say that they require a device < -- cpu sync. i don't know if it's because of the sync, but in practice i am able to compute spectral decompositions for a batch of matrices using cupy interop via dlpack approximatley 10 times faster than with pytorch - and this includes a custom autograd function that also implements backprop for me (500x100x100 tensor, of 500 matrices of size 100x100). i am assuming that if cupy does it quickly, then nvidia already provided some primitives to do it quickly. so i am asking, if it's possible, to use them in pytorch internally as well. alternatives continue using cupy/dlpack interop. additional context i am working on researching trainable models whose nonlinear activations are matrix eigenvalues. making it fast will significantly accelerate my experiments without any special cupy hacks. cc

clean_title: torch.compile mode='reduce-overhead' regression: 7x slower cudagraphlaunch and new cudagraph trees.py overhead in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] has a 40-55% throughput regression in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9 on an h100 gpu running a 6b parameter production gpt inference engine. the root cause is 7x slower [inline code] calls and new python-level overhead in [inline code] that does not exist in 2.9. we performed a controlled isolation experiment with three configurations to separate the pytorch code regression from the cuda toolkit change, and found both pytorch 2.10's inductor code and the cuda 13.x toolkit contribute , with pytorch 2.10's code changes being the primary factor. environment - gpu : nvidia h100 80gb hbm3 (modal cloud) - pytorch versions tested : 2.9.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu130 - cuda toolkits tested : 12.8, 13.0/13.1 - os : ubuntu 24.04 (container) - python : 3.10 - workload : 6b parameter gpt model, [inline code] , 160 concurrent requests, batched inference throughput regression three-way isolation experiment (4 rounds x 500 steps, round 0 discarded, 3 scored) | configuration | med tok/s | stddev | cv | regression | | torch 2.9.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 8,016 | 160 | 2.0% | baseline | | torch 2.10.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 6,639 | 474 | 7.1% | -17.2% | | torch 2.10.0+cu130 (cuda 13.0) | 4,792 | 233 | 4.9% | -40.2% | the isolation experiment (same cuda 12.8, different pytorch) confirms pytorch 2.10's code is responsible for at least 17% throughput loss independent of cuda version. per-round stability | config | r0 (discard) | r1 | r2 | r3 | | torch 2.9 + cuda 12.8 | 8,122 | 7,873 | 8,189 | 7,984 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 12.8 | 7,782 | 7,129 | 6,606 | 6,182 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 13.1 | 5,313 | 5,046 | 4,743 | 4,588 | notable : both torch 2.10 configs show round-over-round degradation (declining throughput over sequential measurement windows) that torch 2.9 does not exhibit. this suggests state accumulation in the cuda graph tree management code. root cause: [inline code] + [inline code] perfetto traces captured with [inline code] show: 1. [inline code] is 7x slower per call | metric | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 41.25 ms | 293.27 ms | +611% | | [inline code] per call | 0.84 ms | 5.99 ms | +7.1x | | launch count | 49 | 49 | same | | graph instantiations | 1 | 1 | same | all 49 launches shifted from the [0.5-1.0) ms range (96% in 2.9) to the [5.0-10.0) ms range (100% in 2.10). 2. new python overhead in [inline code] | component | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 758 ms | 2,866 ms | +278% | | [inline code] avg/event | 0.124 ms | 0.471 ms | +280% | | [inline code] avg | 4.41 ms | 7.02 ms | +59% | 3. functions that appear in 2.10 but not in 2.9 traces | function | count | total time | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.76 ms | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.36 ms | these are entirely new overhead that did not exist in the 2.9 code path. what is not the cause - graph structure : same number of graphs (1), same launch count (49), same instantiation count (1) - gpu kernels : the actual cuda kernels (gemm, attention, triton fusions) are slightly faster in 2.10 - cuda toolkit alone : keeping cuda 12.8 while upgrading to torch 2.10 still shows 17% regression - compile mode : tried [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] — none recover performance. the [inline code] baseline remains the best strategy for both versions. reproducer the workload is a production gpt model, but the issue should be reproducible with any model using [inline code] with cuda graphs. the key characteristics: [code block] expected behavior [inline code] should take roughly the same time per call in 2.10 as in 2.9 ( 0.84ms), and the new [inline code] code paths should not add 667ms of python overhead per 50 inference steps. additional context - the round-over-round degradation pattern (throughput declining across sequential measurement rounds without container restart) in 2.10 but not 2.9 suggests the cuda graph tree management may be accumulating state or doing increasing work per replay - we have full perfetto traces for both versions available on request - our production system serves 1m+ dau and we are blocked from upgrading to 2.10 due to this regression cc

all_comments_text: why are you filing this issue against pytorch and not against nvidia? cudagraphlaunch is a direct call to nvidia apis. |SEP| hi ! thanks for filing the issue, let me take a look at it. as there are many variables at play here, would you be able to share a more specific repro and/or the traces? |SEP| i've investigated concerns about slow cudagraphlaunch() recently. your slowness is likely due to profiling overhead. basically, as soon as you subscribe to cupti activity api for cudagraphlaunch(), cudagraphlaunch() does o(n) work. meanwhile, it is expected to do o(1) work normally. see figure 2 [here]( [url] feel free to try out measuring the length of cudagraphlaunch() on the cpu side to confirm. i would expect that cudagraphlaunch() takes about the same time regardless of pytorch 2.9 vs 2.10 or cuda 12.8 vs cuda 13.1 when you don't use profiling. you can do this with ebpf, or simply just do time.time() before and after graph.replay() (note that replay() launches two extra kernels in addition to cudagraphlaunch(), but i wouldn't worry about that overhead). unfortunately, i am a bit clueless on the pytorch profiler's implementation, so it's unclear to me whether you can simply disable cupti while still getting useful metrics from the pytorch profiler. (in nsys, you can just do [inline code] , for example, to capture only nvtx ranges, but not any cuda api calls). regarding cuda graph trees, you have to give more detail if you want actual help. i don't know what cudagraph trees.py(2029) refers to without an exact commit hash. even then, what you've said is not that helpful. |SEP| there's an integration point to swap in a faster cuda graph wrapper; vllm uses it. check it out. |SEP| i checked with offline and it seems we cannot repro the issue in smaller examples. a repro would be very helpful to further debug. there's an integration point to swap in a faster cuda graph wrapper; vllm uses it. check it out. please check [url] for integration example. it achieves on par or better performance than vllm's default cudagraph. |SEP| -logic you didn't conduct a logical audit on this throughput collapse, claude code did, and as such your comment violates our contributing policy (see [url] none of the copy-paste you did even remotely explains why cudagraphlaunch would become slower, and cudagraph trees didn't have any substantive changes between 2.9 and 2.10 [url] what would be the exact commit you would refer to as introducing extra overhead? no extra pytho-side validation would have any effect on cudagraphlaunch duration. |SEP| after removing the dependency of flashinfer in our prod, the combo of torch 2.10 + python 3.12 + cuda 13.1 outperforms torch 2.9 by 4%. thank you for everybody chiming in. closing for now.

all_comments_text: confirmed this repros with inductor but not [inline code] |SEP| hi after testing, i found that when [inline code] or [inline code] (which behave the same), eager and aot eager decomp partition produce the same index output, while inductor produces different results. however, when [inline code] , the index outputs from eager, aot eager decomp partition, and the inductor backend are all identical. this seems consistent with the semantics of unstable sort. eager and inductor likely use different implementations of unstable sort. i wonder whether we actually intend to require identical index outputs between eager and inductor in the unstable case? cc: |SEP| aten.sort with stable=none or stable=false is meant to be unstable sorting, so we won't be able to guarantee that the ordering match between eager and inductor. (this gist shows that with stable=false, their results still differ [url] which is the intended behavior.)
