clean_title: armv8.1 lse regression for armv8.0 devices
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug pytorch 2.10.0 crashes on armv8.0 processors (cortex-a53/a72/a73) due to lse (large system extensions) instructions in libc10.so. this is a regression - issue reported this problem and claimed it was fixed in 2.4.1, but the issue has returned in 2.10.0. environment • hardware: arm cortex-a53 (armv8.0 architecture) • cpu features: fp asimd evtstrm aes pmull sha1 sha2 crc32 cpuid (notably missing lse) • os: debian trixie (testing), linux 6.12.63 • architecture: aarch64 (arm64) • pytorch version: 2.10.0 • installation method: pip (pip install torch) • python version: 3.13 error description any attempt to import or use pytorch results in sigill (illegal instruction) due to lse atomic instructions that are not supported on armv8.0 processors. error message illegal instruction (core dumped) gdb backtrace program received signal sigill, illegal instruction. 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so (gdb) bt 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe2755d0 in mi process init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe27560c in mi process load () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbf7df180 in call init (env= , argv= , argc= , l= ) at ./elf/dl-init.c:74 call init (l= , argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:26 0x0000ffffbf7df2b8 in dl init (main map=0xffffbf7ff040, argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:121 0x0000ffffbf7fa660 in dl start user () from /lib/ld-linux-aarch64.so.1 disassembly at crash point (gdb) x/5i $pc = 0xffffbe27f3c8 < mi options init+1064 : ldaddal x1, x2, [x0] ; ← lse instruction (sigill) 0xffffbe27f3cc < mi options init+1068 : cmp x2, x0 0xffffbe27f3d0 < mi options init+1072 : b.ne 0xffffbe27f014 < mi options init+100 0xffffbe27f3d4 < mi options init+1076 : adrp x0, 0xffffbe465000 0xffffbe27f3d8 < mi options init+1080 : add x0, x0, x9c8 the ldaddal instruction is part of the armv8.1 lse extension and is not supported on armv8.0 processors like cortex-a53. analysis lse instruction count in libc10.so $ objdump -d /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so | \ grep -e 'ldadd|ldclr|ldeor|ldset|swp|cas' | wc -l 22 the library contains 22 lse instructions, making it completely incompatible with armv8.0 processors. root cause: mimalloc dependency the crash occurs in mi options init() which is part of mimalloc (microsoft's memory allocator). pytorch's c10 library links against mimalloc-static: from c10/cmakelists.txt: if(use mimalloc) target link libraries(c10 private mimalloc-static) endif() it appears that the mimalloc build included in the pytorch wheel is compiled with lse instructions enabled, likely via -march=armv8.1-a or -march=armv8-a+lse flags. steps to reproduce 1. use any armv8.0 processor (cortex-a53, a57, a72, a73 - common in raspberry pi 3/4, many sbcs, older servers) 2. verify cpu lacks lse: grep -o 'atomics\|lse' /proc/cpuinfo (should return nothing) 3. install pytorch 2.10.0: pip install torch 4. try to import: python3 -c "import torch" 5. observe sigill crash regression information this is a regression from the fix mentioned in issue : • original issue: reported lse incompatibility on armv8.0 • claimed fix: said to be resolved in pytorch 2.4.1 • regression: the problem has returned in pytorch 2.10.0 the lse instructions should not be present in builds targeting general arm64 compatibility. the standard -march=armv8-a (without +lse) should be used for maximum compatibility. versions collecting environment information... pytorch version: n/a is debug build: n/a cuda used to build pytorch: n/a rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: debian gnu/linux 13 (trixie) (aarch64) gcc version: (debian 14.2.0-19) 14.2.0 clang version: could not collect cmake version: version 3.31.6 libc version: glibc-2.41 python version: 3.13.5 (main, jun 25 2025, 18:55:22) [gcc 14.2.0] (64-bit runtime) python platform: linux-6.12.63+deb13-arm64-aarch64-with-glibc2.41 is cuda available: n/a cuda runtime version: could not collect cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: could not collect nvidia driver version: could not collect cudnn version: could not collect is xpu available: n/a hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: n/a caching allocator config: n/a cpu: architecture: aarch64 cpu op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit byte order: little endian cpu(s): 4 on-line cpu(s) list: 0-3 vendor id: arm bios vendor id: arm limited model name: cortex-a53 bios model name: cpu @ 0.0ghz bios cpu family: 2 model: 4 thread(s) per core: 1 core(s) per cluster: 4 socket(s): 1 cluster(s): 1 stepping: r0p4 cpu(s) clean_title: cuda error on rtx 5050: no kernel image is available for execution on the device
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i recently purchased an nvidia rtx 50xx (blackwell architecture) gpu. when running any pytorch cuda operation, i get the following error: cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device this happens even with the simplest cuda test, and also when trying to run stable diffusion (comfyui / a1111). it seems pytorch does not yet include compiled cuda kernels for blackwell gpus. please add official support for nvidia blackwell architecture as soon as possible. versions pytorch version: latest stable (pip install torch) cuda version: 12.x (from nvidia driver) nvidia driver version: 591.74 gpu: nvidia rtx 5050 (blackwell) os: windows 11 cc

all_comments_text: additional technical detail: nvidia rtx 50xx gpus use the new blackwell architecture with compute capability sm 120. it seems that current pytorch binaries are not compiled with support for sm 120, which results in: "cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device" please confirm whether sm 120 support is planned for upcoming pytorch releases, and if so, provide an estimated timeline. |SEP| additional technical details: nvidia rtx 50xx gpus use the new blackwell architecture with compute capability sm 120. it appears that current pytorch binaries are not compiled with support for sm 120, which causes all cuda kernels to fail at runtime. even the simplest cuda operation (e.g., torch.zeros(1).cuda()) results in: "cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device" this strongly suggests that pytorch wheels do not yet include ptx or sass kernels targeting compute capability sm 120. could you please confirm whether official support for sm 120 (blackwell) is planned for upcoming pytorch releases, and if possible, provide an estimated timeline? many users with rtx 50xx gpus are currently unable to run any cuda workloads in pytorch due to this missing architecture support. |SEP| note that you need to install a pytorch + cuda 12.8 wheel to get blackwell support. the 12.6 wheel does not have blackwell support. |SEP| hi -byte please take a look at the following support matrix: [url] for blackwell architectures please use 12.8 or 13.0 builds. |SEP| please use [url] to ask generic questions on how to install pytorch or follow the issues reporting template ( you did not share the information what version of pytorch you've installed nor how you did it ( [inline code] as mentioned in description installs only cpu binaries on windows)

clean_body: triu tril kernel causes hsa status error memory aperture violation on amd mi210/mi300x with fp16 tensors 🐛 describe the bug the [inline code] causes an illegal memory access ( [inline code] ) on amd instinct mi210 and mi300x gpus when computing triangular operations on fp16 tensors. this commonly manifests when running transformer models that create causal attention masks. environment - pytorch version : 2.11.0.dev20260118+rocm7.1 - rocm version : 7.1.52802 - gpus tested : - amd instinct mi210 (gfx90a) - 2x 64gb configuration - amd instinct mi300x (gfx942) - os : linux (ubuntu) - python : 3.12 - how pytorch was installed : pip (rocm nightly wheels) error message [code block] minimal reproducer (pure pytorch) [code block] reproducer with huggingface (real-world use case) the bug was originally discovered when running the opt model for perplexity evaluation: [code block] analysis the demangled kernel name is: [code block] this indicates: - data type : [inline code] (fp16) - index type : [inline code] - operation : [inline code] (template param = true) - vector width : 4 - unknown param : false the issue appears to be in the hip implementation of the triangular kernel when: 1. using fp16 dtype 2. operating on larger tensors (2048x2048 in the attention mask case) 3. the grid size [inline code] suggests a 1d kernel launch for the flattened tensor expected behavior the [inline code] operation should complete without memory access violations, producing a valid upper triangular matrix. additional context - the issue does not occur on nvidia gpus with the same code and similar pytorch versions - using [inline code] instead of sdpa does not help - the error happens early in inference (during the first few forward passes) - both mi210 (gfx90a) and mi300x (gfx942) architectures are affected - the issue was discovered during gptq paper reproduction experiments on amd hardware potential causes 1. out-of-bounds memory access in the hip kernel when computing thread indices 2. incorrect grid/block dimension calculations for the hip backend 3. memory alignment issues specific to the gfx90a/gfx942 architectures 4. issue with the vectorized fp16 path (vector width = 4) workaround attempts 1. using [inline code] - did not help 2. reducing sequence length - may help in some cases but not a real solution 3. using fp32 instead of fp16 - untested, would defeat the purpose of quantization experiments related this may be related to other rocm memory issues but i could not find an existing issue tracking this specific kernel crash. cc -amd (rocm maintainers) --- labels suggestion : module: rocm, module: cuda, triaged

clean_title: [heads-up] pytorch 2.9.x conv3d regression may significantly slow qwen3-vl vision forward
clean_body: this is not a qwen3-vl implementation bug, but an upstream pytorch behavior that may affect qwen3-vl users. we observed that upgrading pytorch from 2.8.x to 2.9.x can cause a 10–20× slowdown in vision-conditioned forward / generation for qwen3-vl, while text-only / llm-only inference remains unaffected. root cause (analysis) qwen3-vl vision patch embedding uses a conv3d layer (model.visual.patch embed.proj). on pytorch 2.9.1 (cuda 12.8), this conv3d may fall back to aten::slow conv dilated3d for certain shapes / gpus, instead of using a fast cudnn kernel. profiling shows this single conv3d dominates the entire vision forward. this behavior has been reported upstream in pytorch and appears to be resolved in pytorch 2.10. practical suggestions - if you see severe vision-side slowdowns, consider staying on pytorch 2.8.x - or upgrade to pytorch 2.10+ - be cautious when benchmarking qwen3-vl vision performance on pytorch 2.9.x posting this as a heads-up so users are aware of the upstream interaction. cc

clean_title: [mps] [inline code] / [inline code] race condition
clean_body: due to limitations of device-side barriers in metal, there is likely a race condition in [inline code] for mps, as mentioned here: [url] cc

all_comments_text: i don't think it's a race condition, or even specific to metal, i.e. cuda (or any other gpu) has similar limit, although it's larger than one for mps |SEP| i'm not 100% sure. at the risk of being too verbose, the way i wrote [inline code] is: one thread is created for each element of the input matrix $a$ (really it can be a batch of matrices, but i'll focus on the single matrix case). then for each iteration in [inline code] , each thread does the following steps: 1. calculate one element of $h i$, which is a matrix of the same size as $a$ which depends only on the values of $a$ and $i$. each thread writes the element to a buffer. this buffer is reused on each iteration. 2. [inline code] 3. calculate one element of a matmul $h {0...i} = h {0...i-1} h i$, where $h {0...i-1}$ is the result from the previous iteration. 4. [inline code] 5. write the calculated element of $h {0...i}$ to the result buffer. the barrier at step 2 is necessary to make sure all elements of $h i$ are written to memory before the matmul is calculated by any of the threads. barrier at step 4 is to make sure all threads wait until the whole matmul is calculated before any threads go ahead to calculate the next $h i$ and overwrite the values in the existing buffer for it before all threads got a chance to read the previous value out. if $a$ (and thus $h i$) has more elements in it than the number of threads in a threadgroup, then to me it seems like the barrier won't properly sync all the threads the way the algorithm requires. or is there something else i'm missing now? if there really is a race, then i think the solution is probably to make only one thread group operate on $a$, and each thread can calculate multiple elements if the number of elements is greater than the maximum thread group size. but of course if $a$ is batched, we can create a separate thread group for each batch. let me know what you think |SEP| i think your concern is valid, and the ambiguity here is exactly where correctness bugs tend to hide. the key question seems to be the synchronization scope, not metal vs cuda per se. [inline code] only provides ordering within a single threadgroup. if [inline code] (and thus [inline code] ) spans more elements than can be covered by one threadgroup, then multiple threadgroups will necessarily participate in producing or consuming [inline code] . in that case, the barriers at steps (2) and (4) cannot guarantee the global ordering that the algorithm assumes. in other words, the algorithm requires: 1. all elements of [inline code] to be fully materialized before any thread computes [inline code] 2. all elements of [inline code] to be fully consumed before any thread overwrites the buffer for [inline code] these are global synchronization requirements. a threadgroup-level barrier is insufficient unless we can prove that a single threadgroup owns the entire matrix (or batch slice). i agree this is not unique to metal — cuda has the same limitation — but cuda implementations typically avoid this pattern by either: 1. enforcing one block per matrix (with each thread handling multiple elements), or 2. introducing kernel boundaries between iterations to obtain a global sync. so if the current mps implementation allows multiple threadgroups to participate in a single [inline code] , then this is very likely a real race, not just a theoretical one. the fact that it may “usually work” would be consistent with a latent synchronization bug. your proposed fix (one threadgroup per matrix, with striding inside the group) seems like the safest correctness-preserving approach, especially for non-batched or small-batch cases. kernel-splitting would also be a correctness-first alternative if performance allows. happy to be corrected if there's a guarantee that [inline code] never crosses a threadgroup boundary — but if not, i think this deserves a fix or at least a documented constraint. |SEP| i wrote the following script to try to find out for sure if there is a race condition: [code block] this calls [inline code] with increasing matrix sizes. for each size, it calls the op 100 times and prints out the largest element-wise error between the mps and cpu results. to try to minimize effects of normal floating point error, the input matrix is the identity matrix with one random element set to 1. this is the output i get when i run it: [code block] as soon as the matrix reaches a size with more than 1024 elements, i start seeing significant errors. the system i ran this on has [inline code] . i'll write a fix and i'll add a test similar to the above |SEP| this is a great repro — the cliff exactly at [inline code] lining up with [inline code] strongly supports the threadgroup-scope barrier mismatch theory. for the actual test case, it might be worth explicitly querying the device limits (e.g., via [inline code] apis or metal) and selecting a matrix size that exceeds [inline code] by a small margin (e.g., [inline code] ), so the test adapts dynamically across different mps devices. also, since race conditions can be elusive, consider running multiple iterations within the test to increase the chance of surfacing nondeterminism in ci environments.

all_comments_text: i have conducted additional testing on pytorch 2.10.0+cu126 to investigate this issue. the experimental results are as follows: test configuration: pytorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 layer: lstm (input size=50, hidden size=50, num layers=1, batch first=true) test modes: mode a: cuda eager (cuda/f32/0000) - baseline mode b: cpu compile (cpu/f32/1000) with inductor fallback random mode c: cuda compile (cuda/f32/1000) with inductor fallback random mode d: cpu eager (cpu/f32/0000) experimental results: [code block] key findings: the issue is not related to compilation: mode c (cuda compile) shows perfect alignment (0% error) with mode a (cuda eager), indicating that torch.compile works correctly on cuda. the divergence is between cpu and cuda implementations: both cpu eager (mode d) and cpu compile (mode b) exhibit a 200% relative error compared to cuda eager, and they produce identical results (1.52e+00), suggesting the issue lies in the cpu implementation itself. severity of the issue: even if the input data represents edge cases or boundary values, a 200% relative error between cpu and cuda implementations is unacceptable. this level of divergence can lead to: error accumulation in deep models with multiple lstm layers final prediction label changes when the same model runs on cpu vs cuda inconsistent behavior across different hardware platforms this is a significant issue that cannot be ignored, as it directly impacts model reproducibility and cross-platform consistency. i can provide additional test data, input files, or any other materials needed for further investigation. thank you for your attention to this matter. |SEP| i tried tweaking the repro to not use compile at all, and i see the same numeric differences across cpu and cuda for eager: [code block] |SEP| thank you for this great contribution! all_comments_text: bro why do you highlight me, i have nothing to do with this project. |SEP| sorry about the ping, my bad! updated the post. |SEP| we have a number of escape hatches here hi -offl - we have a section on this in our programming model! please take a look and let me know if you have questions [url] in general, our suggestion is rewriting your model code as this is a fundamental limitation |SEP| thanks for the reference. i went through the programming model section. in my case, the condition [inline code] is computed inside an intermediate layer , not at the model boundary. because of that, moving the condition outside tracing isn’t feasible without introducing a graph break or restructuring the model. [code block] i also tried torch.cond(), but here the condition affects padding and the overall data flow, which ends up changing the model outputs. since this same .item() pattern is used in many places, fixing it through padding or branch alignment would be very time-consuming. is there any other workaround you’d recommend for handling this kind of data-dependent logic? |SEP| cc for cond suggestions, for dynamic shapes |SEP| the code is not amenable to torch.cond rewriting in a meaningful way. claude verifies my impression: 1. the branches have side effects on python lists (llm pos chunks.append(...)). 2. the pointers (img ptr, vid ptr) are python integers used for indexing. 3. the loop iterates over range(s) with data-dependent control flow. the entire for pos in range(s) loop has iteration-level data-dependent branching. torch.cond replaces a single if/else, but the real graph break here is the loop with data-dependent behavior per iteration. you'd need something like torch.scan or torch.while loop to eliminate the python loop, and even then the variable-length list accumulation is a blocker. the code might need to be re-written entirely in order to make it export/compile friendly (removing the python dynamic control flows with padding/masking). |SEP| thanks for the breakdown. the main issue was the [inline code] dependency, which i was able to resolve by replacing the problematic code with: issue: [code block] workaround: [code block] and as you mentioned i was able to rewrite the code for the issues caused by the python list dependency and dynamic pointers - imp ptr, vid ptr and also few others. regarding the [inline code] loop — since [inline code] is derived from [inline code] , it is treated as a static dimension or a symbolic shape rather than a value calculated dynamically from tensor data. as it is not computed at run time, it remains trace-friendly and didn't pose an issue for the export process. after the changes i was able to export and compile the mode without any graph breaks. appreciate the help and insights. i’m closing this issue for now — please feel free to reopen if further discussion is needed. thanks again! |SEP| glad to hear you got the model successfully compiled - thanks -offl!

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using the [inline code] operators on mps, pytorch raises an exception that they are not implemented, suggesting to use the cpu fallback with [inline code] . however, the [inline code] operators are silently broken when using the cpu fallback, as shown by this reproduction case: [code block] if you run [inline code] , the assertion will fail on every rank except 0, because [inline code] didn't actually modify [inline code] . initially, it seems like this could be fixed by adding mutable aliases to the schema for [inline code] , so [inline code] knows to copy back to the input tensors: [url] however, this does not work as the [inline code] operators can be async: 1. [inline code] has already copied from the cpu tensor back to mps by the time it returns, before the async op is complete 2. the result stored in the returned [inline code] will be the cpu tensor in fact, this does not even reliably work for [inline code] with gloo, since gloo does all operations async and relies on python to handle [inline code] : [url] here's a reproduction case for [inline code] that more clearly demonstrates what's going on: [code block] potential fix a potential fix would be to: 1. add mutable aliases to the schemas for all the [inline code] operators 2. have [inline code] handle [inline code] by wrapping it to do the copies (and return the original tensor) once the original [inline code] is complete however, this is a lot of complexity that probably isn't worth it -- more likely, [inline code] should reject the [inline code] operators (and ideally gloo could natively support mps 🙂) versions [code block] cc -huang

all_comments_text: seems related to [url] |SEP| i think this is covered in [url] |SEP| "i’d like to clarify that the core issue is the inconsistency of the error state across different execution modes. observed divergence: for the same fp32 input, the cpu produces a finite result ($3.15 \times 10^{38}$) while cuda produces nan. inconsistent behavior: as noted in pytorch documentation regarding extremal values (e.g., intermediate overflows in norm()), while results may vary, the existence of the error state itself should be consistent. switching execution settings (cpu vs. cuda) changes whether an error occurs at all, which violates backend parity. this functional discrepancy goes beyond 'slightly different results' and warrants a re-evaluation based on the requirement for consistent behavior between backends."

clean_title: [dtensor]inconsistent random number generation in torch.distributed.tensor.randn
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug bug description: when using torch.distributed.tensor.randn to create dtensors with fixed random seeds, the generated tensors are inconsistent between single-gpu and multi-gpu scenarios. specifically, creating a (4, 4) tensor on a single gpu produces different results compared to creating (2, 4) tensors on two gpus separately, even when using the same random seed. expected behavior: with the same random seed, the distributed tensor creation should maintain consistency with single-device semantics. the concatenation of multi-gpu results should match the single-gpu result when using equivalent global shapes and seeds. actual behavior: the random values generated in distributed mode differ from single-device mode, breaking reproducibility and debugging capabilities. versions torch 2.9.1 cuda 12.4 python scripy [inline code] cc -huang -l

all_comments_text: this behavior is kinda expected given the current implementation, since each rank computes a starting offset based on its shard's position. a workaround would be to generate random number on a particular rank and then redistribute. is there any better way to handle this than documenting and maybe a warning? -si repro code: [code block] |SEP| yes, the current behavior is actually the expected behavior, but we do have a project in the works to build the version you want. can you tag your pr here? |SEP| we current have the pr [url] and [url] that uses sharding spec to map tensor/dtensor elements to random number generation and achieve consistent results between single device and multi device when we set the flag(otherwise random generation will not be affect). we are still exploring a way to fix this, we do not yet have an eta.

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug about two weeks ago i updated pytorch with the nightly build that i usually do every week or two. my system was working fine, not a single issue besides the one-off quirks you usually see. but after the update, i found that comfyui was failing to load any models. at first i thought it was the comfyui update that broke it, so i started there, but couldn't find any issue. the only message it was giving me was a seg fault when trying to load the model. i then turned to pytorch and found that it would seg fault during a basic torch command. [code block] i've provided the valgrind, rocminfo, and boot entries. another odd thing is that rewinding the version, doesn't seem to fix the issue. i went back as far as a month and i got the exact same seg fault. another thing i should mention is that i did do a system update just before updating pytorch, so it could be possible that this could have happened from an amd driver update and looking in the wrong spot. if i was smart, i would have tested torch after doing the system update before updating torch, but i have done this so many times, that i got used to it just working. versions [collected env.txt]( [url] [rocminfo.txt]( [url] [valgrind results.txt]( [url] [boot options.txt]( [url] cc -amd

clean_title: triton wheels don't have hashes in urls
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug currently the triton wheels url format in the pytorch index doesn't include hashes in the same format as the wheels for torch. this results in tools like uv not storing hashes in the lockfile, which is a supply chain security concern. torch: [code block] triton: [code block] note that in the torch url the actual href ends with [inline code] , while that is missing from the triton urls. clearly in the triton case the sha is still computed since it's in these other metadata fields, but i guess that isn't part of the server spec (assuming uv is doing the right thing here). some of the torch urls don't have this, so maybe newer torch wheels have this issue too? more context from [pep503]( [url] the url should include a hash in the form of a url fragment with the following syntax: = , where is the lowercase name of the hash function (such as sha256) and is the hex encoded digest. versions current index cc

clean_title: vllm on cpu crashes on models re-runs with torch 2.10
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug while updating pytorch version in vllm from 2.9.1 to 2.10 we noticed that after the first run, vllm/pytorch would try to load the compiled model which does not happen successfully and causes the engine to crash. please see: [url] for reproducer and stack trace. we hot-fixed this in vllm by we temporarily disabling aot compile for cpus in [url] i'm raising this issue here for us to make sure that root cause is fixed. i'm still not sure if the fix should be in vllm or in pytorch versions torch 2.10, on aarch64/x86 cpus cc -arm

clean_title: torch.compile(dynamic=true) specializes batch dimension for layernorm backward, causing recompiles
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary: when using [inline code] with [inline code] to trace a module containing [inline code] and [inline code] , and then compiling the resulting fx graph with [inline code] , the batch dimension ( [inline code] ) gets specialized. this causes a new recompilation for every new batch size, triggered by a "guard size mismatch at index 0". this behavior is specific to the presence of the [inline code] operator in the graph. a forward-only [inline code] graph compiles with a truly dynamic batch dimension as expected. background: i am working on a machine learning force field project and aim to accelerate my model's training process using torch.compile. briefly, the machine learning force field model reads distance vectors between atoms and outputs energy and forces, where the latter are derived by computing the gradient of energy with respect to the distance vectors. in other words, the forward() function of my model contains manually invoked autograd operations. additionally, the loss function of my model consists of two components (energy and forces), which means that higher-order gradients need to be computed during the backward pass of training. meanwhile, the "batch size" varies for each batch (since the number of atoms in each molecule is different), which requires that the code generated by compilation does not statically fix the dimensions of the input tensors. to address this, i first traced my computation graph using make fx(mode="symbolic"), then compiled the model with torch.compile(dynamic=true). however, i found that the compiled output still has statically fixed dimensions, leading to frequent recompilation when feeding inputs of different shapes. after analysis, i identified that the root cause of the recompilation is the backward operator of torch.nn.layernorm. a minimal reproducible example is provided below: minimal reproducer: [code block] [tl out.zip]( [url] error logs expected behavior: with [inline code] , the compiled model should handle a dynamic batch dimension ( [inline code] ) without recompiling. the first run (e.g., [inline code] ) should produce a compiled artifact where the batch dimension is treated as a [inline code] . subsequent runs with different [inline code] values (20, 30, etc.) should reuse this artifact without triggering any guard failures related to the batch dimension. actual behavior: for each new batch size [inline code] , a recompilation is triggered. the logs show a series of guard failures indicating that the batch dimension was specialized to the previous value. for example, when running with [inline code] after running with [inline code] through [inline code] , the logs show: v0120... clean_title: [onnx] gridsample nodes lose all metadata props when exporting with dynamo=true and opset = 20
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when exporting a model with torch.onnx.export(..., dynamo=true), gridsample onnx nodes have no metadata props at all (no name scopes, class hierarchy, stack trace, etc.) when using opset = 20. the same export with opset 16-18 correctly propagates all metadata. this is a regression - the fx graph capture has full source information, but the onnx translation for aten.grid sampler → gridsample drops it for newer opsets. [code block] output [code block] expected behavior gridsample nodes should have metadata props (including pkg.torch.onnx.name scopes, pkg.torch.onnx.class hierarchy, pkg.torch.onnx.stack trace) for all supported opset versions, same as other ops like gemm. actual behavior opset 16, 17, 18: gridsample has full metadata ✅ opset 20, 22: gridsample has zero metadata props ❌ impact this breaks tooling that relies on onnx node metadata to map nodes back to pytorch module hierarchy (e.g., for profiling, debugging, visualization). additional context the report=true diagnostic output shows the fx graph has correct source annotations for grid sampler, but these are not propagated to the onnx gridsample nodes for opset = 20. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0+cu128 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: 12.8 cudnn version: 90701 python version: 3.10.11 (main, may 7 2023, 19:26:31) [clang 16.0.3] cuda available: true gpu: nvidia geforce rtx 4090 os: ubuntu 22.04.4 lts (x86 64) cc

all_comments_text: what’s broken is that torch.compile (torchdynamo + inductor) miscompiles dtype-changing view() operations by treating them as safe aliasing views, even though they reinterpret storage at the byte level. which is why compiling an unrelated bf16 path or adding a “no-op” reshape changes the q6 k result (the compiled kernel ends up reading misaligned or stale bytes, yielding 0.0625 instead of 1.0) the proper fix in pytorch is to treat dtype-changing views as unsafe, either force a materializing copy (or alignment/lifetime barrier) when view(dtype) changes element size, or tighten alias analysis so inductor cannot reuse storage across such reinterpret casts. a practical workaround is to avoid view(dtype) under torch.compile: replace it with to(dtype) (copy), or insert clone() / contiguous() right after any dtype reinterpretation, or keep gguf dequant code out of compiled regions; all of these prevent buffer reuse and restore correctness at the cost of a small copy. |SEP| does this diagnosis and proposed fix sound correct to you? should [inline code] that changes element size be treated as unsafe in inductor (copy-on-view or a no-alias/lifetime barrier), rather than a normal aliasing view, to prevent cross-graph buffer reuse and miscompilation in cases like gguf dequant? |SEP| [inline code] (like [inline code] in c++) is different from [inline code] (like [inline code] ), so it needs to be separately implemented in [inline code] . let's see how the pytorch developers plan to implement this. currently i'm working around this using explicit bit manipulation: [url] |SEP| this seems like inductor issue i have tried with aot eager inductor decomps backend and the accuracy passes, can you take a look? this is high prio, not just because of incorrect results, but also because it affects comfyui which relies on torch.compile to speedup image-video gen models. |SEP| i did root cause and added debug on above repro code. my observation is that some shapes in compile of qk 6 while we are using factory wrapper like (wrap compile) are created as dynamic shape. as compile think it is an extension of earlier compile. if i do add dynamo reset in between 2 compiles the code works as expected. i think this issue can be seen anywhere if someone is using factory wrapper but different closures. the fix is to add hashed closures as property in class codeid so that whenever we use different closures we generate different codeid. and , do let me know your opinion on the root cause and fix. |SEP| yes it happens only when a closure is used. if i remove [inline code] and directly call [inline code] , it does not happen. |SEP| cc , i tested this only reprod with dynamic shapes. thus the recompile. can you take a look ? |SEP| added this to the bug bash to look at next week |SEP| removing myself from assignees. i think i might have been added by mistake. please feel free to re-assign me if my attention is needed. |SEP| torchdynamo generates identical cache keys (codeid) for factory-wrapped functions (like wrap compile) because it hashes the code object without hashing the bound closure variables, causing the second compilation (dequantize blocks q6 k) to falsely hit the cache of the first (dequantize blocks bf16) and execute with stale static assumptions (e.g. wrong shape/stride constants for view(dtype)). [code block] |SEP| -logic , attached pr does similar work. can you review the attached pr? |SEP| -in code review & audit: pr first off, excellent root-cause analysis. you successfully traced a very stealthy cache-poisoning bug back to its origin: dynamo treating dynamically generated functions (closures) as identical because it was only hashing the code object. the added test case (test factory created functions separate pgo state) is bulletproof and perfectly isolates the issue. however, while the fix works for this specific reproducer, there are a couple of system-level edge cases regarding python's cell semantics and distributed caching that we need to harden before this gets merged into main. here is my audit: 1. the valueerror blast radius (cell empty exception) in python, accessing c.cell contents on an uninitialized cell doesn't return none; it raises valueerror: cell is empty. in your implementation: [code block] because the try...except wraps the entire tuple comprehension, if even one cell in the closure is uninitialized, the exception is thrown, the comprehension aborts, and closure hash silently falls back to none. this leaves the function vulnerable to the exact same cache collision bug we are trying to fix if it happens to have mixed initialized/uninitialized cells. fix: handle the valueerror on a per-cell basis. 2. the ephemerality of id() and remote pgo in get closure content: [code block] using id(content) is a classic trap in caching layers. id() is tied to the memory address of the object, which is strictly tied to the lifecycle of the current python process. if a user is leveraging remote pgo (which relies on codeid to serialize state across distributed nodes or disk caches—see automatic dynamic remote pgo in pgo.py), id() will result in non-deterministic hashes across different runs. this will cause remote cache misses and trigger silent recompilations. fix: if the object isn't a code object, attempt to hash its actual value (if it is immutable/hashable) before falling back to id(). we want to preserve determinism as much as possible for remote caches. suggested code refactoring here is a hardened version of your logic that handles both the per-cell valueerror and improves the determinism of the hash: [code block] summary the pr is fundamentally correct in its approach. update the valueerror handling to prevent the whole hash generation from aborting. patch id() to prioritize actual object hashing for remote cache determinism. drop these updates in, and from an engineering perspective, this is a beautiful fix to a nasty bug. great job tracking this down!

all_comments_text: cc |SEP| i am working on it. would try to come up with a fix soon |SEP| i have raised the pr for this issue. ptal |SEP| we have [inline code] to skip dynamic shapes in cudagraph regions. we could similarly add a skip for unbacked shapes only. but i'm not sure the current behavior is a bug ? there's nothing unsafe about taking in an unbacked int. cc |SEP| i agree. but if this unbacked int is data dependent, we need a way to make any operations related to it cudagraph unsafe.. in the vllm use-case specifically, we don't want to skip all dynamic sizes as we mark the batch size as dynamic and then we'd be skipping the whole graph. we might also transition the batch size to unbacked in the future so if we do that we need a way to distinguish between the batch size (cudagraph safe) and the split point (cudagraph unsafe). the most general solution would be to have a way of "tagging" unbacked and dynamic ints as cudagraph safe/unsafe. that way users can directly decide which dims are cudagraph safe and which aren't. |SEP| yes, agreed we should add an api for tagging particular symints. in this instance, it's perhaps unprofitable, but it's not unsafe, since we add guards/respecialize for each unique input. |SEP| this might be due to vllm dropping guards but where does the specialization happen? because we did not see it happen and so the answer was wrong because the data-dependent shapes got locked into cudagraphs. hoping to discuss adding this api at the thursday sync. |SEP| there is a mismatch between pt cudagraph tree and vllm cudagraphwrapper the graph partition signature is [code block] pt2 cudagraph tree collects all ints in the arguments ( [inline code] in this example) and find the corresponding cudagraph. [url] in vllm, we wrap cudagraphwrapper on [inline code] . it does not check all ints in the arguments (thus ignoring [inline code] ). it assumes the only dynamic shape is batch size and only checks the [inline code] (aka batch size). [url] going forward, we can mark an unbacked symint as cg unsafe and exclude all related ops. |SEP| 2.11 milestone for discussion |SEP| cc any thoughts on an api that marks specific [inline code] s as cudagraph-unsafe? |SEP| if we are good with adding the ability to mark specific symints as cudgraph-unsafe, has bandwidth to implement the feature. |SEP| api that marks specific [inline code] s as cudagraph-unsafe? the idea is fine for me,but, can we use terminology other than safe / unsafe for dynamic shapes that are allowed or not allowed in cuda graphs? it seems like everything is “safe” in the sense of correctness, but inductor will perform dynamic dispatch on dynamic shapes and recapture the graph. what we want is to control when to allow dynamic shapes in a cuda graph. maybe something like cudagraph capturable? for example: torch. dynamo.set cudagraph capturable(s0/u0, true/false) also, just for clarification: when s0 is not cudagraph capturable, we will skip the entire graph containing it if graph partition=false. if graph partition=true, we will split the graph—i.e., find the maximal subgraph that does not include s0 and cuda-graph that part this is something that happens automatically in inductor, right, and not something vllm has to handle manually? is my understanding correct? |SEP| torch. dynamo.set cudagraph capturable(s0/u0, true/false)? my concern is that users may not know which s0/u0 to mark. it might be fine for s0 as [inline code] . but it's a bit hard to know exactly the name as u0 (or some other number like u1728). maybe it's easier to mark a custom op such that all of its output symints are cudagraph unsafe? when s0 is not cudagraph capturable, we will not skip the whole graph that have it if graph partition=false, if graph partition=true we will wsplit it, namely find the maximal subgraph that does not include s0 and cuda-graph that part. iiuc this config should only have impact on inductor graph partition. inductor graph partition identifies all ops that use these cg-unsafe symints and splits these ops outside graph partitions. -turnansky thanks for help! i have a pr to potentially fix the issue since there are some api design. |SEP| we also need to think when this annotations happens, the easiest idea would be to mark the symint as cudagraph safe/unsafe upon creation (or included/excluded - sorry laith). but i guess then we're mixing concerns a little bit |SEP| i feel the confusion here mostly stems around the fact that we occasionally do support dynamic shapes with cudagraphs (batch size, by retracing the cudagraph for multiple sizes). in principle, if you had a cuda graph unsafe op that returned a tensor of size u0, the extent processing this u0 could be cudagraphed if you were willing to record cudagraphs for multiple distinct values of u0. is this actually a good idea? well with multiple symbols you will quickly get an explosion of possible combos, so maybe not. if you only care about identifying the batch dynamic symbol, life is relatively simple: there is one symbol which is ok to cudagraph and all the other symbols are not ok. the api here ought to be related to whatever is also setting up the dispatch/record multiple graphs per size. |SEP| if the batch size is the only “cuda graph–safe” dynamic dimension, then we can add an argument to mark dynamic and mark unbacked as well, and disallow everything else. are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? |SEP| are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? not that i know of, because we currently have a linear amount of cudagraphs and with any other size we'd get a quadratic amount which is unsustainable. the only other case would be if a symint is constrained to the batch size but hopefully then we can express it in terms of batch size directly. for example, if we want to pad the batch size to a multiple of 4 for specific ops, we'd want to make sure that works correctly and doesn't get ops excluded from cudagraphs. |SEP| are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? not that i know of, because we currently have a linear amount of cudagraphs and with any other size we'd get a quadratic amount which is unsustainable. the only other case would be if a symint is constrained to the batch size but hopefully then we can express it in terms of batch size directly. for example, if we want to pad the batch size to a multiple of 4 for specific ops, we'd want to make sure that works correctly and doesn't get ops excluded from cudagraphs. if we did padding the size will be a function of the batch size, i dont think that would be an issue.

clean_title: torch.compile fails with notimplementederror for mkldnn layout tensor: 'aten::addmm.out' not available for mkldnncpu backend
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary torch.compile raises notimplementederror when executing a compiled model with mkldnn-layout tensor input, while eager mode works correctly. the error occurs because aten::addmm.out is not implemented for the mkldnncpu backend. this prevents users from combining torch.compile optimization with mkldnn tensor format, which is a common use case for cpu performance optimization. root cause analysis the issue is a backend dispatch mismatch between eager and compiled execution: eager mode: when input is mkldnn layout, nn.linear dispatches to mkldnn. linear pointwise which handles mkldnn tensors correctly. compiled mode: inductor generates code using aten::addmm.out, which does not have an implementation for the mkldnncpu backend. the compiled graph should either preserve the mkldnn-specific operator dispatch or convert mkldnn tensors to dense format before using aten::addmm.out. expected vs actual behavior expected behavior: torch.compile should work with mkldnn tensor inputs (same as eager mode), or a clear error should be raised at compile time indicating that mkldnn tensors are not supported. actual behavior: eager mode: works correctly ✓ compiled mode: raises notimplementederror at runtime ✗ fuzzer compliance checklist this bug was discovered by fuzzing. topic: fuzzer (label request) rtol/atol: n/a (runtime crash, not numerical comparison). fp64 ref: n/a (execution crashes before output). fallback random=true: enabled in reproducer. rng seed reset: enabled in reproducer. minimal reproducer [code block] error logs === configuration === x dense: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false x mkldnn: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=true --- dense tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false --- dense tensor: torch.compile --- compiled out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false values match: true --- mkldnn tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=true --- mkldnn tensor: torch.compile --- notimplementederror: could not run 'aten::addmm.out' with arguments from the 'mkldnncpu' backend. this could be because the operator doesn't exist for this backend, or was omitted during the selective/custom build process (if using custom build). 'aten::addmm.out' is only available for these backends: [cpu, cuda, meta, sparsecpu, sparsecuda, ...] versions pytorch version: 2.6.0+cu124 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: 12.4 rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: ubuntu 22.04.5 lts (x86 64) gcc version: (ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1 22.04.2) 11.4.0 python version: 3.10.12 (main, nov 4 2025, 08:48:33) [gcc 11.4.0] (64-bit runtime) python platform: linux-6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-wsl2-x86 64-with-glibc2.35 is cuda available: true cuda runtime version: 12.4 gpu models and configuration: gpu 0: nvidia geforce rtx 3060 laptop gpu nvidia driver version: 546.30 is xnnpack available: true cpu: architecture: x86 64 vendor id: authenticamd model name: amd ryzen 7 5800h with radeon graphics cpu(s): 16 thread(s) per core: 2 core(s) per socket: 8 cc
